source_paper_title: A Fast Temporal Decomposition Procedure for Long-Horizon Nonlinear Dynamic Programming
source_paper_bibtex: @article{Na_2024, title={A Fast Temporal Decomposition Procedure for Long-Horizon Nonlinear Dynamic Programming}, volume={49}, ISSN={1526-5471}, url={http://dx.doi.org/10.1287/moor.2023.1378}, DOI={10.1287/moor.2023.1378}, number={2}, journal={Mathematics of Operations Research}, publisher={Institute for Operations Research and the Management Sciences (INFORMS)}, author={Na, Sen and Anitescu, Mihai and Kolar, Mladen}, year={2024}, month=may, pages={1012–1044} }
context:
Page 3, Introduction (explicit questions Q1 and Q2)

First, only local convergence guarantee is established while global convergence is still unknown.
In fact, as we will explain later, the Schwarz scheme does not converge globally in general. There is no guarantee that the composed solution of the subproblems is the solution to the full problem, even if we correctly specify the boundary variables for each subproblem. Thus, we arise the question:
Q1: How to design an OTD-based procedure that converges globally?
title: Design OTD-based globally convergent algorithm for long-horizon nonlinear dynamic programs
background_latex:
Consider the nonlinear equality-constrained dynamic program over horizon length \(N\):
\[
\min_{x_0,\dots,x_N,\,u_0,\dots,u_{N-1}} \; \sum_{k=0}^{N-1} g_k(x_k,u_k) + g_N(x_N)
\]
subject to the dynamics
\[
 x_{k+1}=f_k(x_k,u_k) \quad (k=0,1,\dots,N-1), \qquad x_0=\bar x_0,
\]
where \(x_k\in\mathbb{R}^{n_x}\) are states, \(u_k\in\mathbb{R}^{n_u}\) are controls, and \(g_k, f_k\) are given (possibly nonlinear, nonconvex) functions.

An overlapping temporal decomposition (OTD) partitions the stage set \([0,N]\) into exclusive subintervals \([n_i,n_{i+1}]\) with knots \(0=n_0<n_1<\cdots<n_M=N\), and extends each exclusive interval by \(b\ge 1\) stages at both ends to form an extended interval \([m_1^i,m_2^i]\) with
\[
 m_1^i=\max\{n_i-b,0\},\qquad m_2^i=\min\{n_{i+1}+b,N\}.
\]
Associated to each extended interval one defines a subproblem parameterized by boundary (interface) variables \(d_i\) that encode values at or near \(m_1^i\) and \(m_2^i\) (e.g., initial state and terminal state/control/dual information). Solving subproblems in parallel and then composing only the exclusive parts \([n_i,n_{i+1})\) yields a full-horizon candidate trajectory.

A key issue is that even if each subproblem is solved to optimality for given boundary variables, the composed solution need not satisfy the Karush--Kuhn--Tucker (KKT) conditions of the full problem unless compatibility conditions hold at knots (agreement of overlapping solutions and appropriate consistency of multipliers).
problem_definition_latex:
Construct an algorithm based on overlapping temporal decomposition of the horizon into extended intervals with overlap size \(b\), which generates iterates \((x^{\tau}_{0:N},u^{\tau}_{0:N-1},\lambda^{\tau}_{0:N})\) for the full-horizon problem and is globally convergent in the sense that, from an arbitrary (not necessarily locally accurate) initialization, the algorithm is guaranteed to converge to a KKT point of the full nonlinear dynamic program (under explicitly stated, verifiable assumptions).

In particular, the goal is to specify:
1) how the subproblems are parameterized and solved across iterations,
2) how subproblem solutions are composed into a full-horizon iterate,
3) what globalization mechanism (e.g., merit function/line search/trust region/coordinator) is used,
so that global convergence to a KKT point is guaranteed for general long-horizon nonlinear dynamic programs of the above form.
latex:
Consider the nonlinear equality-constrained dynamic program over horizon length \(N\):
\[
\min_{x_0,\dots,x_N,\,u_0,\dots,u_{N-1}} \; \sum_{k=0}^{N-1} g_k(x_k,u_k) + g_N(x_N)
\]
subject to the dynamics
\[
 x_{k+1}=f_k(x_k,u_k) \quad (k=0,1,\dots,N-1), \qquad x_0=\bar x_0,
\]
where \(x_k\in\mathbb{R}^{n_x}\) are states, \(u_k\in\mathbb{R}^{n_u}\) are controls, and \(g_k, f_k\) are given (possibly nonlinear, nonconvex) functions.

An overlapping temporal decomposition (OTD) partitions the stage set \([0,N]\) into exclusive subintervals \([n_i,n_{i+1}]\) with knots \(0=n_0<n_1<\cdots<n_M=N\), and extends each exclusive interval by \(b\ge 1\) stages at both ends to form an extended interval \([m_1^i,m_2^i]\) with
\[

\]
Associated to each extended interval one defines a subproblem parameterized by boundary (interface) variables \(d_i\) that encode values at or near \(m_1^i\) and \(m_2^i\) (e.g., initial state and terminal state/control/dual information). Solving subproblems in parallel and then composing only the exclusive parts \([n_i,n_{i+1})\) yields a full-horizon candidate trajectory.

A key issue is that even if each subproblem is solved to optimality for given boundary variables, the composed solution need not satisfy the Karush--Kuhn--Tucker (KKT) conditions of the full problem unless compatibility conditions hold at knots (agreement of overlapping solutions and appropriate consistency of multipliers).

Construct an algorithm based on overlapping temporal decomposition of the horizon into extended intervals with overlap size \(b\), which generates iterates \((x^{\tau}_{0:N},u^{\tau}_{0:N-1},\lambda^{\tau}_{0:N})\) for the full-horizon problem and is globally convergent in the sense that, from an arbitrary (not necessarily locally accurate) initialization, the algorithm is guaranteed to converge to a KKT point of the full nonlinear dynamic program (under explicitly stated, verifiable assumptions).

In particular, the goal is to specify:
1) how the subproblems are parameterized and solved across iterations,
2) how subproblem solutions are composed into a full-horizon iterate,
3) what globalization mechanism (e.g., merit function/line search/trust region/coordinator) is used,
so that global convergence to a KKT point is guaranteed for general long-horizon nonlinear dynamic programs of the above form.
location_in_paper: Page 3, Introduction (explicit questions Q1 and Q2)
open_problem_quote:
First, only local convergence guarantee is established while global convergence is still unknown.
In fact, as we will explain later, the Schwarz scheme does not converge globally in general. There is no guarantee that the composed solution of the subproblems is the solution to the full problem, even if we correctly specify the boundary variables for each subproblem. Thus, we arise the question:
Q1: How to design an OTD-based procedure that converges globally?
counterexample_suitability_rationale: The question asks for a constructive globally convergent algorithm rather than the validity of a specific universal inequality. There is no clear finite-dimensional instance space whose search would decisively refute the existence of such a procedure in general; negative results would depend on formal impossibility frameworks not specified in the statement.
alphaevolve_suitability_rationale: Candidate algorithms (e.g., interface-update rules, merit functions, line-search/trust-region coordinators) can be represented programmatically and tested on families of benchmark NLDPs with computable stationarity/feasibility residuals as a fitness signal. However, empirical performance is only a proxy for a global convergence guarantee, so automated search can suggest designs but cannot certify the required theorem-level property.

verification.notice: Generated from the paper text only and not independently verified as still open.

literature_review.synthesis:
The open problem asks for an OTD-based algorithm for long-horizon nonlinear dynamic programs that is globally convergent from arbitrary initialization to a KKT point, including explicit subproblem parameterization, composition, and a globalization mechanism. The paper "A Fast Temporal Decomposition Procedure for Long-horizon Nonlinear Dynamic Programming" provides such an algorithm (FOTD) by embedding overlapping temporal decomposition into an SQP framework. The key design choice is to apply OTD not to the original nonlinear subproblems solved to optimality (as in the overlapping Schwarz scheme), but to the linear-quadratic dynamic program that represents the SQP/Newton step. Subproblems are posed on extended intervals with overlap \(b\), with a terminal quadratic penalty \(\mu\) to ensure well-posedness, and are solved in parallel; their exclusive parts are composed to form an inexact Newton direction.

Globalization is achieved via a differentiable exact augmented Lagrangian merit function \(\mathcal L_\eta(z,\lambda)\) and an Armijo line search. Using exponential decay of sensitivity for dynamic programs, the inexact Newton direction produced by OTD is shown to satisfy a relative error bound \(\|(\tilde\Delta z-\Delta z,\tilde\Delta\lambda-\Delta\lambda)\|\le C\rho^b\|(\Delta z,\Delta\lambda)\|\). With \(b\) large enough (so \(\delta=C\rho^b\) is small) and penalty parameters \(\eta_1,\eta_2\) chosen to make the direction a descent direction for \(\mathcal L_\eta\), global convergence to stationarity of the KKT system follows, hence convergence to a KKT point under standard constraint qualification and second-order conditions. Locally, the method accepts unit steps and achieves uniform linear convergence with rate \(C\rho^b\), matching the earlier local theory for the overlapping Schwarz method while avoiding repeated nonlinear subproblem solves.

The broader literature supplies the two pillars used in this solution: (i) exponential decay of primal-dual sensitivity in nonlinear dynamic programming, which explains why overlap controls interface inconsistency; and (ii) globally convergent SQP/Newton globalization mechanisms based on exact differentiable augmented Lagrangians or related merit/filter strategies. Remaining gaps are largely practical/engineering rather than theoretical: reducing the need for separate Hessian-modification and subproblem factorizations, extending the theory cleanly to inequality constraints and active-set identification, and exploring trust-region or filter globalizations within the same OTD-inexact-Newton framework.

sources.title: A Fast Temporal Decomposition Procedure for Long-horizon Nonlinear Dynamic Programming
sources.theorem_or_result: Introduces FOTD: an SQP method with an exact augmented Lagrangian merit function and an overlapping temporal decomposition (OTD) approximation of the Newton system. Proves global convergence to KKT points under reduced-Hessian lower bound, controllability, boundedness, and compactness assumptions, with overlap size \(b\) and penalty parameters chosen so the inexact direction remains a descent direction. Establishes local acceptance of unit steps and uniform local linear convergence with rate \(C\rho^b\).
sources.connection: Directly answers the open problem by providing an OTD-based algorithm (FOTD) with a globalization mechanism (exact augmented Lagrangian + Armijo line search) and a proof of global convergence to KKT points for long-horizon nonlinear dynamic programs.

sources.title: On the convergence of overlapping Schwarz decomposition for nonlinear optimal control
sources.theorem_or_result: Analyzes an overlapping Schwarz (OTD-style) scheme for nonlinear optimal control: subproblems on extended intervals are solved to optimality and composed on exclusive parts. Proves uniform local linear convergence with rate \((C\rho^b)^\tau\) under controllability and boundedness-type assumptions, but does not provide a global convergence guarantee.
sources.connection: Provides the key local convergence theory and motivates why a globalization mechanism is needed; FOTD builds on this by embedding OTD into SQP with a merit-function line search to obtain global convergence.

sources.title: Exponential decay in the sensitivity analysis of nonlinear dynamic programming
sources.theorem_or_result: Establishes exponential decay of primal-dual sensitivity for nonlinear dynamic programs under reduced-Hessian positivity, controllability, and boundedness assumptions: perturbations at a stage affect optimal primal/dual variables at distant stages by at most \(C\rho^{\text{distance}}\).
sources.connection: Supplies the mathematical mechanism that makes OTD viable: overlap \(b\) yields exponentially small interface error \(O(\rho^b)\), enabling both the Schwarz local rate and FOTD’s inexact-Newton error bound used in global convergence.

sources.title: Exponentially accurate temporal decomposition for long-horizon linear-quadratic dynamic optimization
sources.theorem_or_result: For long-horizon linear-quadratic (convex) dynamic optimization, analyzes OTD and proves exponentially accurate recovery of the full-horizon solution from subproblem solutions: stagewise error bounded by \(C\rho^b\) under controllability and boundedness conditions.
sources.connection: Provides foundational OTD theory in the convex LQ setting that inspired later nonlinear Schwarz/FOTD developments; clarifies how overlap controls truncation/interface error.

sources.title: Superconvergence of online optimization for model predictive control
sources.theorem_or_result: Studies online optimization/MPC and proves superconvergence properties leveraging decay of sensitivity and structured KKT systems; provides technical tools on KKT inverse decay and perturbation propagation in dynamic optimization.
sources.connection: Supplies technical lemmas (e.g., decay structure of KKT inverse) used in analyzing truncated-horizon Newton steps and local error recursions, closely aligned with OTD-based algorithm analysis.

sources.title: Scalable nonlinear programming via exact differentiable penalty functions and trust-region newton methods
sources.theorem_or_result: Develops scalable nonlinear programming methods using exact differentiable penalty/augmented Lagrangian constructions and trust-region Newton steps, with global convergence guarantees under standard assumptions.
sources.connection: Provides broader optimization context and justification for using differentiable exact augmented Lagrangian merit functions (as in FOTD) to globalize Newton/SQP-type methods while retaining good local behavior.

sources.title: Line search filter methods for nonlinear programming: Motivation and global convergence
sources.theorem_or_result: Establishes global convergence of filter line-search methods for nonlinear programming, foundational for modern large-scale NLP solvers such as IPOPT.
sources.connection: Represents an alternative globalization mechanism (filter) to the merit-function line search used in FOTD; relevant when considering other globally convergent coordinators for decomposed SQP methods.
